summary: Validated observed implementation only. This record now has machine-checkable VM proof for the raw CPU idle-state registry bundle, but Microsoft still does not publish it as a supported control surface.

setting.name: CPU Idle States Bundle
setting.casual_explanation: CPU idle states let Windows save power when the processor has little to do. This tweak tries to reduce or bypass that behavior for latency-focused systems.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it when chasing minimum latency for gaming, audio work, or other timing-sensitive workloads.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Reducing idle-state behavior can increase responsiveness in some narrow cases, but it can also raise heat and power consumption. The current problem is that the app uses raw power-manager registry values instead of a clearly supported Microsoft control surface.
setting.professional_notes: The app writes DisableIdleStatesAtBoot, IdleStateTimeout, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power. The local Power.txt trace also lists DisableIdleStatesAtBoot in the power-values block. This review found Microsoft documentation for processor idle states as a feature area, but not a primary Microsoft registry mapping that validates this exact raw bundle as a supported configuration method.

allowed_values.label: Primary-source mapping not confirmed
allowed_values.meaning: The feature area is real, but this review did not find a primary Microsoft registry source validating this exact raw bundle.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes a raw CPU idle-state registry bundle, but this review did not find a primary Microsoft registry surface that validates it as a supported tuning method.

windows_defaults.label: Windows-managed processor idle baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems using normal processor idle-state management

states.rationale: This review did not elevate the raw registry bundle to a published Windows baseline.

windows_defaults.notes: Processor idle behavior should be treated as Windows-managed unless a supported control surface is confirmed.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems using standard Windows processor power management
avoid_for: None until a supported Microsoft mapping is confirmed

states.rationale: Keep processor idle behavior under the normal Windows-managed baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want supported Windows processor power management behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to force undocumented raw registry tuning.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps its normal idle-state logic.
The app's raw bundle remains documented but not validated.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Introduction to Processor Idle States
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents processor idle states as a Windows power-management feature area, but this source does not publish the raw registry bundle used by the app.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableIdleStatesAtBoot, IdleStateTimeout, and ExitLatencyCheckEnabled under the Power key.

evidence.title: nohuto power trace for DisableIdleStatesAtBoot
evidence.summary: Power.txt lists DisableIdleStatesAtBoot in the power values block, matching the first element of the app's raw idle-states bundle.

evidence.title: Repo power notes
evidence.summary: The repo tracks these power-manager values as research notes, but this review did not find a primary Microsoft registry mapping for publishing them as a validated preset.

decision.why: The VM probe captured the exact raw registry bundle written by the app and restored the baseline cleanly. The record remains apply_allowed false because Microsoft still does not publish this as a supported control surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: DisableIdleStatesAtBoot=1; IdleStateTimeout=0; ExitLatencyCheckEnabled=1 -> restored to null
validation_proof.notes: Guest VM probe captured the exact before/after/restored registry bundle and returned the machine to baseline.
